justification: All six pass criteria met: (1) Context coupling explicitly identified — Bird calls out 'breaching the existing context boundary' and the need for an anti-corruption layer. (2) Data ownership question raised — BR-3 states Warehouse is sole owner of StockLevel, and the escalation discusses who owns 'available stock' via the read-model vs source-of-truth distinction. (3) TOCTOU/simultaneous-order race condition identified — BR-4 and AC-3 directly address two concurrent orders for the last item, with a testable assertion. (4) Stock reservation vs check distinction surfaced — 'Reservation' is called out as 'NOT YET IN THE UBIQUITOUS LANGUAGE but almost certainly needed', and the first escalation explicitly distinguishes reject vs hold vs queue. (5) Escalation to MJ for architectural coupling pattern — three escalations cover the integration pattern (direct API vs domain events vs saga), with explicit anti-corruption layer recommendation. (6) New cross-context invariant stated — BR-2 defines the order-cannot-be-confirmed-if-stock-insufficient invariant, and BR-4 defines the concurrency invariant.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough bounded context analysis — identifies that 'Product' means different things in each context
TOCTOU is not just mentioned but has a concrete testable assertion (two orders, StockLevel=1, at most one confirmed)
Reservation TTL expiry edge case identified — phantom reservations permanently reducing available stock
Correctly flags that showing stale 'in stock' then blocking at checkout is worse than no indicator at all
Low confidence score (45) is well-calibrated given the numerous ambiguities in the feature request
Identifies Warehouse downtime as a new operational risk for order placement — fallback policy needed
Finance/accounting impact noted — blocked vs cancelled orders have different revenue recognition treatment

agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A feature request to integrate two previously independent bounded contexts — Warehouse Management (stock) and Sales/Order Management (orders) — by introducing a real-time stock check that blocks orders when inventory is insufficient. This is a classic cross-context coupling challenge in domain-driven design.","bounded_context":"Cross-cutting: Warehouse Management × Sales/Order Management.
